subtopics.subtopic_title: ADOLESCENT PCOS DIAGNOSIS AND METABOLIC CO- MANAGEMENT
subtopics.concept: Adolescent polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) often overlaps with obesity and insulin resistance, but overdiagnosis is a concern when adult diagnostic criteria are applied prematurely. During normal pubertal transition, irregular menstrual cycles and acne can occur, making age-appropriate diagnostic thresholds essential. International evidence-based guidelines highlight that irregular cycles within the first year post-menarche are typically normal. A definitive diagnosis of adolescent PCOS requires both persistent menstrual irregularity, interpreted in relation to years post-menarche, and evidence of clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, after excluding mimicking disorders. Pelvic ultrasound is not recommended for diagnosis within eight years post-menarche, and anti-Müllerian hormone testing should not be used in adolescence. Key differential diagnoses include thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, and nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which should be excluded based on phenotype and specific testing when indicated. When PCOS is suspected in an obesity clinic, particularly in cases of hirsutism or severe acne combined with persistent menstrual irregularity, metabolic risk evaluation should take precedence over cosmetic concerns. This includes measuring blood pressure, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and considering an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) if the patient is at high risk. Lipid profiles and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) should also be assessed, as steatotic liver disease may coexist. Definitive management begins with structured lifestyle interventions and weight management as first-line approaches. For symptom control, combined oral contraceptives are commonly prescribed for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism, following standard contraindication screening protocols. Metformin is not a cosmetic or anti-obesity agent but may be considered in cases of dysglycemia or significant insulin resistance, ideally under specialist guidance. Psychological distress, including depression and body image concerns, is closely linked to PCOS, necessitating routine screening and integrated mental health support during follow-up. Sampling pitfalls, such as premature reliance on pelvic ultrasound or anti-Müllerian hormone testing, should be avoided. Clinicians must interpret diagnostic criteria cautiously, ensuring they are age-appropriate and exclude mimicking conditions to avoid overdiagnosis.

case_studies.title: Adolescent PCOS Suspected in Obesity Clinic
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl presents to a rural health clinic with her mother, complaining of irregular periods and excessive facial acne for the past 18 months. She attained menarche at age 12 and reports cycles occurring every 45–60 days, with occasional skipped months. Her mother is concerned about her recent weight gain, noting a 10 kg increase over the past year despite no significant changes in diet or activity. On examination, the girl has a BMI of 29 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile), moderate hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score: 10), and acanthosis nigricans on the neck and axillae. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. Laboratory results reveal fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL and ALT of 45 IU/L. Her thyroid function tests are normal. She denies galactorrhea or significant hair loss. The clinician considers further workup to exclude mimicking disorders and evaluates her metabolic risk profile.

subtopics.subtopic_title: COMBINED ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE SELECTION AND THROMBOSIS RISK COUNSELING IN PCOS
subtopics.concept: In adolescents with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), the selection of combined oral contraceptives (COCs) for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism requires careful consideration of thrombosis and cardiovascular risks. Obesity, a common comorbidity in PCOS, increases the baseline risk of venous thromboembolism (VTE), necessitating a thorough risk assessment before prescribing COCs. Key factors to document include smoking or vaping status, history of migraine with aura, personal or first-degree family history of VTE, prolonged immobilization, known thrombophilia, prior stroke, uncontrolled hypertension, severe liver disease, and concurrent use of medications that reduce COC effectiveness, such as rifampicin. Blood pressure must be confirmed during the visit, as uncontrolled hypertension is a contraindication to estrogen-containing contraception. Counseling should emphasize that COCs do not address insulin resistance or weight trajectory; lifestyle modifications and, when indicated, metformin remain the cornerstone of metabolic management. For safe formulation selection, prioritize COCs with the lowest effective estrogen dose, typically 20–35 micrograms of ethinyl estradiol, as higher estrogen doses are associated with increased thrombosis risk. Progestin choice should balance androgenic effects to manage acne and hirsutism while considering local availability and cost. Follow-up visits are essential to reassess bleeding patterns, headaches, mood changes, and blood pressure, as well as to screen for early thrombotic symptoms, such as leg swelling, chest pain, or sudden dyspnea. If contraindications to estrogen-containing methods arise or the patient cannot use estrogen safely, switching to a progestin-only method is recommended, with coordination involving gynecology or adolescent medicine specialists. Shared decision-making should be documented, as treatment goals vary among patients, encompassing cycle control, acne management, contraception, or a combination of these. This individualized approach ensures that the therapeutic plan aligns with the patient’s needs while minimizing risks associated with COC use in the context of PCOS.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and PCOS: COC Selection and Thrombosis Risk Counseling
case_studies.vignette: A 16-year-old girl presents to a rural outpatient clinic in India with complaints of irregular menstrual cycles and worsening facial acne over the past year. She has a BMI of 32 kg/m² and reports significant weight gain since entering puberty. She denies smoking or vaping, and there is no personal or family history of thromboembolic events. Her mother has type 2 diabetes. She is otherwise healthy and not on any medications. On examination, she has mild hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score of 10) and acanthosis nigricans over the neck. Blood pressure is 128/84 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement. The patient expresses concern about her appearance and is seeking a solution for her irregular periods. She is open to using medications but is unaware of the risks or benefits. A focused discussion is initiated to assess her candidacy for combined oral contraceptives (COCs) while addressing her concerns about weight and acne.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess thrombosis and cardiovascular risks in adolescents with PCOS before prescribing combined oral contraceptives, and counsel on lifestyle modifications and follow-up care.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and PCOS: COC Selection and Thrombosis Risk
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a rural outpatient clinic in India with concerns of irregular menstrual cycles and persistent acne for the past year. She has a BMI of 32 kg/m² and reports weight gain over the last two years. She denies smoking or vaping. Her mother has a history of deep vein thrombosis during pregnancy. She has no history of migraines, prolonged immobilization, or prior thrombotic events. On examination, she has moderate acne on her face and hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score of 10). Blood pressure is 128/84 mmHg. The patient expresses frustration with her appearance and requests treatment for her acne and irregular periods. She has not tried any medications previously and is not on any other treatment. Her family is concerned about the potential risks of hormonal medications and asks for detailed counseling about the safety of contraceptive pills.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess thrombosis risk and counsel on combined oral contraceptive selection in adolescents with PCOS and obesity.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and COC Selection in Adolescent PCOS
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a pediatric clinic in a semi-urban Indian setting with complaints of irregular menstrual cycles and worsening acne over the past year. She has a BMI of 32 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for her age and gender) and a strong maternal family history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. On detailed questioning, she denies smoking, vaping, or any history of migraines. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism (VTE). Her blood pressure is 122/80 mmHg, and she has no signs of hirsutism on examination. She has not been on any medications but was recently advised lifestyle modifications for weight management during a nutritionist visit. The patient and her mother inquire about starting 'hormonal tablets' for her menstrual irregularities and acne. They express concerns about potential side effects, including weight gain and blood clots, based on information from friends and social media. The patient reports no recent immobilization, and there is no history of liver disease. A discussion about her options is initiated.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess thrombosis risk and counsel on safe combined oral contraceptive (COC) selection in an adolescent with PCOS and obesity.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and COC Selection in Adolescent PCOS
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a rural health clinic with her mother, reporting irregular menstrual cycles and worsening acne over the last year. She has a BMI of 32 kg/m², and her mother mentions that she has gained significant weight since puberty. The patient denies any smoking, vaping, or use of medications. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism (VTE), stroke, or thrombophilia. She does not report migraines or aura symptoms. On examination, she has moderate acanthosis nigricans over her neck and axillae, and her blood pressure is 128/84 mmHg, measured twice during the visit. She has no signs of hirsutism or virilization. The family is concerned about her irregular periods and acne and asks about starting medication for both issues. The patient is otherwise healthy and has no history of prolonged immobilization, liver disease, or chronic conditions. Labs from two months ago showed normal liver function tests and fasting blood glucose. The mother requests clarification on risks associated with hormonal treatments and whether they would affect her weight further.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess thrombosis and cardiovascular risks in adolescents with PCOS and obesity before initiating combined oral contraceptives, and counsel on safe options and lifestyle modifications.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and Obesity Management in PCOS: COC Selection and Thrombosis Risk Counseling
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a rural outpatient clinic in India with complaints of irregular menstrual cycles and excessive hair growth on her face and arms for the past year. Her mother reports that she has gained significant weight over the last two years, with a sedentary lifestyle and frequent consumption of fried snacks and sugary drinks. On examination, her BMI is 32 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for her age), and she has acanthosis nigricans around her neck. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. She denies smoking, vaping, or a history of migraines. There is no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, stroke, or thrombophilia. The patient is seeking advice on regulating her periods and addressing her hirsutism. Laboratory tests from a prior visit show elevated total testosterone and mild dyslipidemia. She has not tried any medications for her symptoms previously. Her mother expresses concerns about potential side effects of hormonal treatments, particularly regarding blood clots, and asks for clarification on risks and benefits.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess thrombosis risk and counsel on safe combined oral contraceptive (COC) selection in an adolescent with PCOS and obesity.

subtopics.subtopic_title: IHBLT DOSE PRINCIPLES AND STAGED LIFESTYLE ESCALATION
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: Intensive health behavior and lifestyle treatment (IHBLT) is the cornerstone of pediatric obesity management, emphasizing structured, family-based, multicomponent interventions delivered consistently over time rather than relying on brief counseling or singular diet plans. According to the AAP Clinical Practice Guideline, IHBLT should be delivered over a duration of 3–12 months, with evidence indicating a threshold of at least 26 contact hours for meaningful outcomes, while higher doses, such as 52 or more hours, yield better results. Monthly or quarterly advice is insufficient, necessitating frequent touchpoints with measurable goals and homework assignments. Indian guidance aligns with this staged escalation approach, progressing from basic counseling (prevention plus) to structured weight management (goal-oriented follow-up), comprehensive multidisciplinary intervention (team-based, high-contact care), and, when necessary, tertiary care interventions. Escalation within lifestyle treatment should be based on documented non-response to prior stages rather than clinician frustration, and pharmacotherapy should only be considered after exhausting lifestyle-based options. Given time constraints in many Indian settings, the recommended "dose" of IHBLT can be delivered through brief but frequent visits, group sessions, and telehealth reinforcement, ensuring consistency and structured delivery. Each contact should focus on a defined module, address a specific barrier, and measure a process indicator to track progress effectively. This approach operationalizes lifestyle therapy as a longitudinal, high-contact intervention rather than sporadic advice. The Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) framework emphasizes that consistent escalation within lifestyle treatment is critical to achieving sustainable outcomes in pediatric obesity management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: NUTRITION AND MEAL-STRUCTURE MODULES FOR INDIAN HOUSEHOLDS
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: The "Nutrition and Meal-Structure Modules for Indian Households" provide a structured approach to managing overnutrition and obesity in paediatric outpatient practice. Screening and testing guidance for specific thresholds, doses, durations, sensitivity, or specificity values are not specified in the source. Prophylaxis and vaccination recommendations are also not mentioned. The module-based framework emphasizes practical, culturally relevant strategies to improve nutrition without relying on calorie counts, focusing instead on liquid calorie reduction, ultra-processed snack minimization, meal timing adjustments, and the school food environment. The first module establishes shared framing and baseline routines, aiming to reduce stigma by treating obesity as a chronic condition with a focus on health and function. Families are encouraged to make small, manageable changes every 2–4 weeks, starting with eliminating sugar-sweetened beverages and replacing them with water or unsweetened drinks, alongside implementing a no-screens-during-meals policy. Monitoring involves tracking beverages consumed daily and meals with screen usage, avoiding restrictive diets that may lead to rebellion or disordered eating risks. The second module targets meal timing and school exposure, advocating for daily breakfast, restructuring late dinners, and introducing one feasible school tiffin change, such as adding protein-rich foods like dal, eggs, curd, paneer, or lean meat, along with fruit. Monitoring includes tracking breakfast days per week and dinner timing, with an emphasis on addressing meal timing and snack access rather than focusing solely on portion sizes. The third module modifies plate-based Indian diet patterns to enhance satiety without relying on calorie calculations. Families are guided to fill half the plate with vegetables, a quarter with protein, and a quarter with whole grains or millets while reducing ultra-processed snacks. Monitoring can be simplified using methods like taking daily plate photos for three days per week. This approach avoids prescribing Western diet charts that may not align with Indian household cooking practices, ensuring better adherence. Sanitation, food, and water hygiene measures, return precautions, and community or outbreak reporting are not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INDIAN HOUSEHOLD PORTION TOOLS LABEL READING AND LOW-COST SWAPS
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: Managing overnutrition and obesity in paediatric outpatient practice requires practical strategies tailored to Indian households, particularly in mixed-meal diets and budget-constrained settings. Families often struggle with implementing healthy eating habits, making it essential to provide concrete tools for portion control and label reading, along with low-cost dietary swaps. For portion control in Indian plates, meals should be anchored with visible protein and vegetable components before adding refined carbohydrates. This typically includes dal, rajma, chana, curd, paneer, eggs, fish, or chicken alongside cooked vegetables or salad, followed by roti or rice as the smaller portion. A practical guideline is to divide the plate into half vegetables, one-quarter protein, and one-quarter roti or rice, adapted to the family’s staple foods. Beverage choices should exclude sugar-sweetened drinks and sweetened health beverages, treating fruit juice as sugar water for weight management. Suitable replacements include water, unsweetened chaas, or plain milk in age-appropriate quantities. Label reading in India should focus on the "per 100 g/100 ml" column rather than serving size. Families should be taught to identify sugar, saturated fat, and sodium content per 100 g and recognize various names for sugar, such as sucrose, glucose syrup, maltodextrin, and fruit concentrate. Products with sugar listed among the first ingredients should be treated as high-sugar items. For special occasions like festivals, birthdays, or hostel food, a relapse plan is more effective than outright bans. Families should agree on planned portions, consume regular meals before events to prevent rebound overeating, and return to routine eating patterns at the next meal without punitive measures. Low-cost dietary swaps can help families make healthier choices without financial strain. Fried snacks like namkeen and biscuits can be replaced with roasted chana, peanuts, seasonal fruits, sprouts, boiled corn, or homemade poha or upma with added vegetables. Cooking methods can shift from deep frying to roasting or steaming where feasible. Progress should be documented by identifying two household-specific swaps per visit, ensuring measurable improvements over time. Not specified in the source are return precautions and community/outbreak reporting.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PHYSICAL ACTIVITY, SLEEP, SCREEN, AND BEHAVIOR MODULES WITH RELAPSE PREVENTION
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept:
After stabilizing nutrition routines in the management of overnutrition and obesity, structured modules addressing physical activity, sleep, screen time, and behavior skills should be implemented, as these domains often influence adherence more than dietary perfection. Physical activity must be prescribed rather than suggested, with a goal of averaging 60 minutes per day of moderate-to-vigorous activity across the week. A low-barrier approach is recommended initially, such as 15 minutes after school and 15 minutes in the evening, with gradual weekly increases. For adolescents, safe strength training should be normalized two to three days per week, incorporating simple exercises like bodyweight squats, wall pushups, and planks alongside aerobic activity to enhance function and insulin sensitivity. Monitoring the minutes per day and days per week of strength sessions is essential, avoiding overly intense plans that may lead to early dropout, particularly in deconditioned teens.

Sleep is a first-line intervention for obesity and should be operationalized through a “sleep prescription” that includes a consistent wake time, a device-off time, and adherence to age-appropriate sleep duration targets: 9–12 hours for children aged 6–12 years and 8–10 hours for adolescents aged 13–18 years. Screen time interventions should address two modifiable behaviors: eliminating screens during meals and avoiding screens for at least 60 minutes before bedtime. Monitoring device-off times and screen-free meals is crucial.

Household behavior skills are integral to success, emphasizing shared responsibility within the home environment. This includes controlling food availability, modeling healthy behaviors by parents, and using rewards based on behaviors rather than weight outcomes. Relapse prevention strategies should anticipate predictable disruptions, such as exams or festivals, by implementing a “minimum viable routine” that combines a sleep cutoff, a beverage rule, and at least 20 minutes of movement. Maintenance visits should focus on reviewing BMI trajectories and comorbidity trends to determine whether to maintain the current approach, intensify the intervention, or escalate care when criteria are met.

subtopics.subtopic_title: AGE-STRATIFIED LIFESTYLE TARGETS FROM PRESCHOOL TO ADOLESCENCE
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: Age-stratified lifestyle targets are essential in managing overnutrition and obesity in paediatric outpatient practice, as they help prevent inappropriate interventions and associated harm. The primary goal across all age groups is to improve health and function, with weight trajectory expectations tailored to developmental stages. In preschool children, diagnosis is based on WHO weight-for-length or weight-for-height standards. Management emphasizes responsive feeding practices and structured daily routines rather than weight-loss diets. Specific targets include avoiding sugar-sweetened beverages, discouraging energy-dense snacking as a soothing mechanism, establishing sleep routines aligned with WHO 24-hour movement guidelines, and promoting active play. Pharmacotherapy is not recommended at this stage. For school-age children, the focus shifts to weight maintenance with a declining BMI trajectory as height increases, alongside improvements in fitness, sleep, and diet quality. This approach minimizes stigma and fosters adherence by demonstrating progress even when weight reduction is gradual. In adolescents, pubertal changes, social pressures, and academic schedules often disrupt habits, necessitating more explicit targets. These include slowing or reversing BMI trajectory, optimizing metabolic markers, improving sleep timing, reducing late-night screen use, and addressing irregular meal patterns. Proactive screening for dysglycemia, steatotic liver disease, depression, and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) in symptomatic girls is recommended. Adolescents with class 2 or class 3 obesity and significant comorbidities should be considered for earlier intervention within a staged framework, with careful monitoring for disordered eating prior to intensifying weight-focused strategies. Severe disease progression in this age group underscores the need for timely escalation of care. These age-specific lifestyle targets provide a structured approach to managing paediatric obesity, ensuring interventions are developmentally appropriate and supportive of long-term health outcomes.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a School-Age Child: Lifestyle Challenges and Targets
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric outpatient clinic by his parents, who are concerned about his rapid weight gain over the past year. The parents report that he has become less active since transitioning to online classes during the pandemic, spending most of his time on screens. He often skips breakfast, consumes sugary beverages daily, and frequently snacks on fried foods while studying. His parents also mention that he struggles to fall asleep before midnight and wakes up fatigued. On examination, his weight is 42 kg (>97th percentile for age and sex), height is 135 cm (75th percentile), and BMI is 23.1 kg/m² (>95th percentile). He has no acanthosis nigricans or other signs of insulin resistance. His blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg (normal for age). The rest of the physical exam is unremarkable. The parents are seeking advice on how to help him adopt healthier habits without making him feel singled out or judged.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand age-appropriate lifestyle targets for school-age children with overnutrition, focusing on gradual BMI trajectory changes, improved diet quality, physical activity, and sleep hygiene while minimizing stigma.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a 10-Year-Old with Poor Lifestyle Habits
case_studies.vignette: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the outpatient clinic by his mother, concerned about his increasing weight over the past year. She reports that he has become less active since transitioning to online classes during the pandemic and now prefers to spend hours playing video games or watching television. His diet includes frequent consumption of fried snacks, sugary beverages, and late-night meals. He has also been complaining of fatigue and difficulty concentrating in school. His sleep schedule is irregular, with late bedtimes and less than 7 hours of sleep per night. On examination, his weight is at the 95th percentile, and his height is at the 50th percentile for his age. His BMI is 24 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for age and sex). A focused exam reveals no acanthosis nigricans or hepatomegaly. No other systemic abnormalities are noted. The mother inquires about whether medications or a strict diet plan would be necessary to address his weight concerns.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand age-appropriate lifestyle targets for managing overnutrition in school-age children, focusing on gradual BMI trajectory improvements, fitness, diet quality, and sleep hygiene without stigmatizing interventions.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a School-Age Child: Lifestyle and Family Dynamics

The family lives in an urban area and has limited access to outdoor play spaces. The mother reports that the boy often snacks on packaged chips and sugary drinks while watching television for 3–4 hours daily after school. He has difficulty falling asleep at night and wakes up feeling tired. His school performance has also declined slightly. On examination, his weight is 42 kg (>97th percentile for age and gender), and his height is 134 cm (75th percentile). His BMI is 23.4 kg/m² (>95th percentile). His blood pressure is 112/74 mmHg (above the 90th percentile for his age). The rest of the physical examination, including skin and abdominal findings, is unremarkable. The mother expresses concern about how to manage his eating habits and activity levels, given their busy family schedule and cultural emphasis on large meals.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a School-Age Child: Lifestyle Challenges
case_studies.vignette: A 9-year-old boy presents to the outpatient clinic with concerns of excessive weight gain over the past 2 years. His mother reports that he spends most of his day indoors, either studying or playing video games, and consumes sugary beverages and packaged snacks daily. She has also noticed that he has difficulty keeping up with peers during physical activities at school and often complains of feeling tired. His sleep routine is irregular, with late bedtimes and frequent use of screens before sleeping. subtopics.subtopic_title: RESPONSIVE FEEDING COUNSELING FOR INFANTS AND PRESCHOOL CHILDREN WITH RAPID WEIGHT GAIN
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: Responsive feeding counseling is a critical component in managing rapid weight gain in infants and preschool children, particularly in the context of overnutrition. Overnutrition in this age group is often linked to feeding patterns rather than an inherently excessive appetite, and timely intervention can prevent its progression into later childhood. When a child under five years exhibits rapid upward crossing of growth percentiles, it is essential to first confirm the accuracy of length or height measurements. The subsequent assessment should focus on feeding routines, sleep patterns, screen exposure, and caregiver responses to fussiness. Common high-yield feeding issues in Indian settings include prolonged bottle use, especially at bedtime, excessive milk consumption displacing solid foods, intake of sugar-sweetened beverages or sweetened “health drinks,” frequent grazing, and screen-assisted feeding. Caregivers should be asked to provide a detailed 24-hour dietary recall, including the timing, context (e.g., who feeds the child and where), and whether distractions such as screens are present. It is also important to differentiate true hunger cues from non-hunger cues like boredom, screen-seeking behavior, or caregiver anxiety. Responsive feeding principles should guide counseling. Caregivers are advised to decide what food is offered and when, while allowing the child to determine how much to eat. Structured meals and planned snacks should be established, sugar-sweetened beverages eliminated, and feeding in front of screens discontinued. For toddlers, total milk intake should be capped at age-appropriate volumes, calorie-laden liquids avoided at bedtime, and water offered as an alternative if a bedtime drink is needed. Specific scripts, such as “We will offer dinner at the table, then one planned bedtime routine; if hungry later, the option is water,” can help reduce conflict. Lifestyle advice should emphasize adequate sleep, daily active play, and minimal screen time, as these are upstream regulators of appetite. While endocrine or genetic causes of rapid weight gain are rare, specialist evaluation is warranted if accompanied by short stature, developmental regression, dysmorphism, or hyperphagia with loss of satiety.

case_studies.vignette: A 2.5-year-old boy is brought to a primary care clinic in a semi-urban Indian town by his mother, who is concerned about his rapid weight gain over the last six months. Sleep duration is about 8 hours per night, with frequent bedtime milk feeds.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MINIMUM-CONTACT AND HYBRID DELIVERY MODELS FOR INDIAN OPD SETTINGS
subtopics.category: INTENSIVE LIFESTYLE TREATMENT DELIVERY IN OUTPATIENT PRACTICE
subtopics.concept: In Indian outpatient department (OPD) settings, delivering intensive lifestyle treatment for overnutrition and obesity management in children and adolescents can be challenging due to limited contact opportunities. However, minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models can still meet the necessary dose principles if structured and frequent follow-through is ensured. Screening begins with an extended baseline visit lasting 20–30 minutes, during which anthropometry and classification are confirmed, comorbidity screening with pre-defined actions is planned, one diet goal and one behavior goal are set, and the first lifestyle module is delivered. This is followed by four to six brief follow-up visits, each lasting 10–12 minutes, scheduled every 2–4 weeks. Early follow-up improves treatment fidelity by leveraging initial family motivation and addressing barriers before routines solidify. Each follow-up visit should include weight measurement, periodic height and BMI trajectory assessment, blood pressure monitoring, review of one process indicator (e.g., sugary-drink days per week, breakfast days per week, minutes of activity per day, or device-off time), delivery of one focused module with written or phone-based homework, and problem-solving counseling to address one barrier. To enhance treatment dose without extending individual visits, monthly group sessions focusing on diet and activity can be incorporated when feasible. These sessions may include label reading, a “tiffin planning” demonstration, or supervised activity starter sessions. Additionally, trained staff can provide reinforcement through phone or WhatsApp communication between visits, and telehealth check-ins can be utilized for interim troubleshooting, particularly during exam months. Distributed contact across these formats can approximate the required treatment dose, provided the program is standardized and tracked. Documentation of module completion and process measures is essential, as escalation decisions should be based on documented nonresponse rather than subjective impressions. Prophylaxis/vaccination guidance, sanitation and food/water hygiene recommendations, return precautions, and community/outbreak reporting are not specified in the source.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition Management in an Adolescent Using Hybrid OPD Models
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old boy presents to an urban Indian outpatient clinic with his mother, concerned about gradual weight gain over the past two years. He reports difficulty participating in sports at school due to fatigue and occasional knee pain. His mother mentions that he spends several hours daily on his phone and prefers fried snacks over home-cooked meals. On examination, his weight is 65 kg, height is 148 cm, and BMI is 29.7 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for his age). His blood pressure is 125/85 mmHg. No acanthosis nigricans or other signs of comorbidity are noted. The family is motivated to address his health but has limited availability for frequent clinic visits due to work and school schedules. The clinic team plans a structured lifestyle intervention using a hybrid approach, including an extended baseline visit for anthropometry, dietary goal-setting, and delivery of the first module, followed by brief biweekly visits and telehealth check-ins. A group session on meal planning is scheduled for next month.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand how minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models can be used for obesity management in resource-constrained OPD settings, emphasizing structured follow-ups and family engagement.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a 12-Year-Old: Minimum-Contact OPD Model
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a busy urban hospital OPD in India with concerns of excessive weight gain over the past year. His mother reports that he has become increasingly sedentary, spending over 4 hours daily on devices, and consuming sugary drinks and snacks frequently. His school performance has declined, and he complains of occasional knee pain after mild activity. On examination, his BMI is above the 95th percentile for his age and gender, and his blood pressure is at the upper limit of normal (118/78 mmHg). There is no acanthosis nigricans or other signs of endocrine pathology. A detailed dietary recall reveals frequent missed breakfasts and reliance on packaged foods. The extended baseline visit is completed, including anthropometry confirmation, comorbidity screening, and setting initial goals for reducing sugary drinks and increasing daily activity by 20 minutes. A follow-up visit is scheduled in 3 weeks to assess progress and deliver the next module, with interim support provided through WhatsApp to address barriers.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the structured approach for initiating and maintaining minimum-contact obesity management in an Indian OPD setting, including baseline screening, goal setting, and interim follow-through techniques.

case_studies.title: Obesity Management in a 12-Year-Old Using Minimum-Contact Model
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl, Priya, presents to a busy pediatric OPD in urban India with her mother. She has gained 8 kg over the past year, and her mother reports that Priya spends most of her time studying or on her phone. Her diet includes frequent fried snacks and sugary drinks. Priya feels tired during physical education classes and avoids outdoor activities, citing joint discomfort. On examination, her weight is 58 kg, height is 148 cm, and BMI is 26.5 kg/m² (>95th percentile for age and sex). Blood pressure is 122/80 mmHg. Acanthosis nigricans is visible on her neck. The physician conducts a 20-minute baseline visit, confirming obesity classification and screening for comorbidities. A diet goal (reduce sugary drinks to 2 days/week) and a behavior goal (15 minutes of daily activity) are set. A lifestyle module on balanced meals is introduced, and the family is scheduled for a follow-up in 2 weeks. Priya’s mother expresses concern about sustaining changes during exam periods.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand how minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models can structure obesity management in resource-constrained settings, emphasizing early follow-up, goal setting, and distributed contact to address barriers.

case_studies.title: Obesity Management in a 13-Year-Old Using Minimum-Contact OPD Model
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old boy presents to a busy urban pediatric OPD in India with concerns about excessive weight gain over the past year. His mother reports that he has been increasingly sedentary since transitioning to online schooling during the pandemic and consumes sugary beverages daily. He also complains of occasional fatigue and difficulty keeping up with peers during physical activities. On examination, his BMI is 28 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for age and sex), and his blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. There is no acanthosis nigricans or other signs of endocrine dysfunction. A detailed dietary recall reveals frequent snacking on packaged foods and skipping breakfast. A screening questionnaire identifies low physical activity (<30 minutes/day) and excessive screen time (>4 hours/day). The family expresses willingness to make changes but is concerned about the feasibility of frequent visits due to their work schedules. Anthropometry and comorbidity screening are completed during the extended baseline visit, and the family is introduced to a hybrid model involving brief OPD follow-ups every 3 weeks, supplemented by WhatsApp-based reinforcement and monthly group sessions.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the structure and implementation of minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models for obesity management in resource-constrained OPD settings, including prioritizing early follow-up, leveraging family motivation, and utilizing distributed contact

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a Teenager: Minimum-Contact Model in an Indian OPD
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old boy presents to a busy urban pediatric OPD in India with his mother, concerned about his weight gain over the past year. His mother reports increased snacking on packaged foods and decreased outdoor play since his school switched to online classes during the pandemic. The boy mentions feeling tired during physical education at school and occasional teasing by peers. His weight is 72 kg, height is 150 cm, and BMI is 32 kg/m², placing him in the obese category. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. Focused examination reveals acanthosis nigricans on the neck and no other acute findings. The family has limited time for follow-up due to the father’s work schedule and upcoming exams for the boy. The pediatrician schedules a 20-minute extended baseline visit, during which anthropometry is confirmed, a diet goal is set (reducing sugary drinks to 2 days per week), and a behavior goal is initiated (30 minutes of device-free outdoor play daily). A simple lifestyle module is delivered, and the family is advised to return in two weeks for a brief follow-up visit. Between visits, WhatsApp-based reinforcement is planned to address barriers like exam stress and snacking habits.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand how minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models can be structured to initiate obesity management in resource-constrained OPD settings.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and Obesity Management in an Indian OPD Setting
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a busy urban pediatric OPD in India with his mother, concerned about weight gain over the past year. His mother reports that he has become less active since transitioning to online schooling during the pandemic and often skips breakfast, consuming packaged snacks and sugary drinks throughout the day. His weight has increased by 10 kg in the last year, and he now complains of fatigue during physical activity and occasional knee discomfort. On examination, his BMI is 28 kg/m², placing him in the obese category for his age, and his blood pressure is mildly elevated at 130/85 mmHg. A focused dietary history reveals frequent consumption of fried foods and minimal intake of fruits and vegetables. The family expresses willingness to make changes but feels overwhelmed by conflicting advice from relatives. The boy’s school schedule and upcoming exams limit his availability for frequent visits. The pediatrician plans a structured approach to address the family’s concerns while minimizing disruption to their routine.
case_studies.learning_objective: Apply minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models to initiate lifestyle-based obesity management in a resource-constrained OPD setting.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in an Adolescent with Limited OPD Access
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old boy from a semi-urban area in India presents to the outpatient department with his mother, concerned about weight gain over the past year. He reports feeling fatigued during physical activities and has started avoiding sports at school. His mother mentions that he spends 4–5 hours daily on his smartphone or watching TV and frequently consumes sugary beverages and fried snacks. His BMI is 28 kg/m² (>95th percentile for age and sex). Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. The extended baseline visit includes anthropometry, classification of obesity, and screening for comorbidities such as hypertension and sleep disturbances. A diet goal (reducing sugary drinks to 2 days per week) and a behavior goal (30 minutes of outdoor activity daily) are set. The first lifestyle module is delivered, focusing on reducing screen time. The mother is encouraged to attend monthly group sessions, but she expresses concerns about their ability to travel frequently due to her work schedule. A follow-up visit is planned in two weeks.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the implementation of minimum-contact and hybrid delivery models for obesity management in Indian OPD settings, including structured goal-setting and follow-through strategies to address barriers in low-resource contexts.

subtopics.subtopic_title: LIFESTYLE ADAPTATIONS FOR NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS AND DISABILITIES
subtopics.category: SPECIAL POPULATION MODIFICATIONS AND SAFETY DOCUMENTATION
subtopics.concept: Children with neurodevelopmental disorders and disabilities are at an increased risk of obesity due to factors such as limited activity options, sensory-based eating preferences, disrupted sleep patterns, and, in some cases, the use of weight-promoting medications. Effective management in this population requires shifting the focus from the child’s willpower to the caregiver and the broader environment. Intensive lifestyle interventions should be simplified and integrated into daily routines. Caregiver-mediated plans are essential and should include visual schedules and consistent cues, such as fixed meal and snack times, a controlled selection of healthy foods with repeated exposure, and environmental restrictions on ultra-processed snacks and sugar-sweetened beverages. For children with sensory-related food aversions, initial efforts should prioritize eliminating high-calorie liquids and introducing one tolerated protein or fruit/vegetable option, rather than attempting comprehensive dietary changes. Physical activity recommendations must align with the child’s motor abilities and safety considerations. Short, frequent movement sessions lasting 5–10 minutes throughout the day are encouraged. For children with tone or joint issues, physiotherapy-guided strengthening exercises are beneficial. Low-injury activities, such as assisted walking, adapted cycling, or water-based exercises, should be prioritized when feasible. Screen time counseling should avoid unrealistic restrictions; instead, the focus should be on protecting sleep by implementing device-off times and preventing eating during screen use. Follow-up care should monitor a small number of caregiver-selected process indicators, such as the weekly consumption of sugary drinks, bedtime consistency, and daily minutes of physical activity. Progress should be reinforced positively and without stigma. When goals are not achieved, the issue should be addressed as a systems problem involving the environment and available supports, with an emphasis on escalating assistance rather than attributing blame to families.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MEDICATION-ASSOCIATED WEIGHT GAIN CO-MANAGEMENT WITH PSYCHIATRY
subtopics.category: SPECIAL POPULATION MODIFICATIONS AND SAFETY DOCUMENTATION
subtopics.concept: Medication-associated weight gain is a significant contributor to pediatric obesity and necessitates coordinated care involving psychiatry rather than isolated management within obesity clinics. Certain medications, including atypical antipsychotics, some antidepressants, mood stabilizers, and chronic glucocorticoids, can rapidly increase appetite and weight. Effective co-management begins with recognition and baseline documentation, which should include weight, height, BMI category, blood pressure, and, ideally, waist circumference at or near the initiation of weight-promoting therapy. Metabolic monitoring, including glucose or HbA1c, lipids, and ALT, should be scheduled based on the specific medication and its associated risk level to enable early detection of dysglycemia and dyslipidemia, both of which are actionable conditions. Collaboration with psychiatry or the prescribing clinician is essential to assess whether the lowest effective dose of the medication is being used and to explore the possibility of switching to a less weight-promoting alternative, provided it is clinically safe. Psychiatric stability must remain the priority, and psychotropic medications should not be stopped or altered without coordination. Concurrently, lifestyle interventions should be initiated immediately, focusing on meal timing, elimination of sugary beverages, regular sleep patterns, and a feasible activity plan, as early lifestyle modifications can mitigate cumulative weight gain. While metformin is not classified as an anti-obesity drug, evidence supports its modest efficacy in attenuating antipsychotic-associated weight gain in certain pediatric populations. The Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) obesity guideline recognizes metformin as an adjunct option in select cases, typically with specialist-linked monitoring. If prescribed, metformin should be initiated at a low dose, titrated gradually, and monitored for tolerability, renal function, and long-term vitamin B12 status. Shared decision-making and a clear monitoring plan should be documented to ensure explicit communication of the benefits and risks involved.

case_studies.title: Medication-Associated Weight Gain in a Teenager on Antipsychotics
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl presents to a pediatric clinic in a semi-urban Indian town with concerns of rapid weight gain over the past 6 months. She has a history of bipolar disorder and has been on olanzapine for mood stabilization for the last 8 months. Her mother reports a significant increase in her appetite, especially for high-calorie snacks, and a lack of interest in physical activity. The family is worried as her school uniform no longer fits, and she has become increasingly self-conscious. On examination, her BMI is at the 98th percentile for her age and sex, and her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. Acanthosis nigricans is noted on her neck. Routine metabolic screening reveals fasting glucose of 104 mg/dL and ALT of 45 U/L. The family expresses concern about continuing the medication but is hesitant due to her improved psychiatric stability. They seek guidance on addressing her weight gain.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the role of coordinated care with psychiatry in managing medication-associated weight gain, including baseline assessment, metabolic monitoring, and lifestyle interventions, while prioritizing psychiatric stability.

case_studies.title: Medication-Associated Weight Gain in a Teenager on Antipsychotics
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl from a semi-urban area presents to the paediatric outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of rapid weight gain. She had been started on olanzapine for bipolar disorder 8 months ago, following several episodes of mood instability. Her parents report that she has gained approximately 12 kg during this period, with increased appetite and frequent snacking on calorie-dense foods. She complains of fatigue but denies any fever, abdominal pain, or other systemic symptoms. On examination, her BMI is 28 kg/m² (above the 95th percentile for her age and sex), and her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg. Waist circumference is 92 cm. Acanthosis nigricans is noted around her neck. Initial lab results show fasting glucose of 102 mg/dL and triglycerides of 190 mg/dL. Psychiatric follow-up has been irregular due to travel constraints, but her parents report significant improvement in her mood and behavior since starting the medication.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and co-manage medication-associated weight gain in collaboration with psychiatry, incorporating baseline documentation, metabolic monitoring, and lifestyle interventions.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MINIMUM DOCUMENTATION ELEMENTS FOR CONTINUITY AND ESCALATION RATIONALE
subtopics.category: SPECIAL POPULATION MODIFICATIONS AND SAFETY DOCUMENTATION
subtopics.concept: In the context of paediatric overnutrition and obesity management, minimum documentation elements for continuity and escalation rationale are essential to ensure safety and effective care. Documentation should explicitly record the anthropometric system used, specifying whether it is the WHO weight-for-length/height chart or the IAP BMI chart, alongside the current weight category (overweight, obesity, class 2 or class 3 obesity) and the BMI trajectory over time rather than relying on a single value. Blood pressure (BP) values must be documented, including the confirmation plan if elevated. Comorbidity screening should be noted as “test plus action,” with dates and values for glucose, HbA1c, lipids, and ALT clearly recorded, along with the next steps for any abnormalities, such as repeat intervals, referral thresholds, or treatment actions. Lifestyle intervention details must include the modules delivered and key process indicators, such as beverages consumed per week, breakfast days, daily minutes of activity, and device-off time. This allows future clinicians to differentiate between non-response to treatment and inadequate intervention delivery. For escalation, eligibility criteria and shared decision-making processes must be documented. When pharmacological treatment is initiated, records should include baseline contraindication screening, the dosing and titration plan, the monitoring strategy, and the stopping rule. For bariatric surgery referrals, documentation must include the severity class, associated comorbidities, the duration and intensity of structured lifestyle interventions, and the rationale for surgery referral. To ensure continuity of care and avoid stigmatization, person-first, non-stigmatizing language must be used throughout the documentation. Employing a standardized template aligned with IAP and global guidelines facilitates seamless transitions across outpatient, inpatient, and school-health settings, ensuring consistent and effective management of paediatric obesity.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition & Obesity Management: Continuity Documentation in LMIC Context
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy from an urban low-income setting presents to the outpatient clinic with his mother, concerned about weight gain over the past two years. His mother reports that he has been less active since school shifted to online classes during the pandemic and has developed a preference for sugary beverages and fried snacks. The boy has also started experiencing occasional knee pain and difficulty keeping up with peers during physical activities. On examination, his BMI is 29 kg/m², plotted on the IAP BMI chart, placing him in the obesity category. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, requiring confirmation at a follow-up visit. Anthropometric trends from previous visits show a steep upward trajectory in BMI over the past year. Screening labs from three months ago revealed an HbA1c of 6.1%, borderline fasting glucose of 105 mg/dL, and ALT of 45 U/L, with no lipid panel performed. Lifestyle counseling was initiated at that time, but adherence details are not documented. The mother requests further advice, expressing concern about long-term health risks. The clinic team considers the need for escalation and documentation updates to ensure continuity of care.

case_studies.title: Continuity and Escalation Documentation in Paediatric Obesity Management
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old girl presents to a community health clinic in an urban area with her mother, concerned about weight gain over the past year. Her mother reports increasing fatigue and difficulty participating in physical activities at school. The child’s diet includes frequent sugary beverages and snacks, and she spends over 4 hours daily on screens. Her weight trajectory from prior visits shows a steady increase, with a BMI now above the 97th percentile on the IAP BMI chart, placing her in the obesity category. Blood pressure measured today is 130/85 mmHg, above the 95th percentile for her age and height, with no prior values documented. Focused examination reveals acanthosis nigricans on the neck and axillae but no signs of acute distress. Laboratory tests from 6 months ago showed fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL and ALT of 45 U/L, but no follow-up was documented. The family has attended two lifestyle counseling sessions but reports difficulty adhering to recommendations. The clinician is considering escalation of care and discusses next steps with the family.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the minimum documentation elements required for continuity and rationale when escalating care in paediatric obesity management, including anthropometric trends, comorbidity screening, lifestyle intervention adherence, and shared decision-making

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and Obesity: Continuity and Escalation in a 12-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a rural outpatient clinic with his mother, concerned about his increasing weight over the past two years. He reports fatigue during physical activities and occasional knee pain. His mother mentions that he spends over 4 hours daily on screens and skips breakfast most mornings. On examination, his weight is 68 kg and height is 148 cm, with a BMI of 30.9 kg/m² (obesity class 2 per IAP BMI chart). His blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement. Laboratory results from two weeks ago show fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL, HbA1c of 6.1%, and ALT of 55 U/L. Lipid profile was not performed. The mother notes no prior structured lifestyle interventions. The clinic has limited access to specialists, and the family expresses concern about affordability of follow-up care. The physician discusses a plan for lifestyle changes but considers escalation due to the boy’s comorbidities and lack of prior intervention. Documentation includes anthropometric trends, BP confirmation, comorbidity screening results, lifestyle habits, and shared decision-making notes for possible pharmacological or surgical referral.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the minimum documentation elements required for continuity and escalation in paediatric obesity management.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition in a 13-Year-Old: Documentation for Escalation
case_studies.vignette: A 13-year-old boy presents to a community health center in urban India with complaints of fatigue and difficulty participating in physical activities during school. His mother reports significant weight gain over the past two years, despite attempts to reduce portion sizes at home. On focused examination, his weight is 72 kg, height is 150 cm, and BMI is 32 kg/m², placing him in the class 2 obesity category per IAP BMI charts. Blood pressure is measured at 130/85 mmHg, with no prior records available. Screening labs from two weeks ago show fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL, HbA1c of 6.2%, and ALT of 45 U/L, but lipid profile results are pending. The family reports inconsistent adherence to lifestyle advice, including irregular breakfast consumption and less than 20 minutes of daily activity. No pharmacological treatment has been initiated yet. The clinician notes no documentation of device-off time or beverage intake patterns. The mother asks if further tests or treatments are needed, and the clinician considers whether escalation is warranted given the trajectory of BMI and comorbidities.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the minimum documentation elements required for continuity of care and escalation rationale in paediatric obesity management.

case_studies.title: Overnutrition and Obesity Management in a 12-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to the paediatric outpatient clinic in an urban hospital with concerns of weight gain over the past year. His mother reports difficulty in managing his diet due to frequent consumption of sugary beverages and snacks after school. He has been less active since transitioning to online classes during the pandemic. His BMI is recorded at 28 kg/m², placing him above the 95th percentile on the IAP BMI chart for his age and sex. Blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, confirmed on repeat measurement. Focused examination reveals acanthosis nigricans on the neck and no other acute findings. Laboratory results from three months ago show fasting glucose of 106 mg/dL and ALT of 45 IU/L, but no follow-up testing has been conducted. Lifestyle intervention records are sparse, with no documentation of activity levels or dietary patterns. The family expresses concern about his increasing fatigue and occasional headaches but denies any acute complications. The paediatrician considers the need for escalation based on his weight trajectory, comorbidities, and lack of structured intervention.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify the minimum documentation elements required for continuity and escalation rationale in paediatric obesity management, including anthropometric trends, comorbidity screening, lifestyle intervention details, and decision-making processes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VISIT-ONE DIAGNOSTIC CONFIRMATION AND INITIAL TREATMENT CONTRACT
subtopics.category: SEQUENCED OUTPATIENT PATHWAY AND FOLLOW-UP CADENCE
subtopics.concept: The first outpatient visit for overnutrition and obesity management should serve as both a diagnostic confirmation and the initiation of longitudinal care, rather than a simple “screen and advise” session. The process should follow a structured, sequential approach to ensure decision-readiness. Begin by confirming anthropometric measurements and classifying severity using the appropriate growth chart. If height or weight values appear unexpected, re-measure to ensure accuracy. Calculate the BMI and plot it on the relevant chart: the WHO weight-for-length/height chart for children under 5 years and the IAP 2015 BMI charts for children aged 5–18 years. Document overweight (adult-equivalent BMI of 23) or obesity (adult-equivalent BMI of 27), and when necessary for escalation planning, classify as class 2 or class 3 obesity using the IAP percent-of-cutoff approach. Next, perform etiologic triage to differentiate primary obesity from other causes. While most cases are primary, early-onset severe obesity accompanied by hyperphagia, poor linear growth, cushingoid features, or hypothalamic symptoms warrants targeted endocrine or genetic referrals rather than routine panels. Avoid broad endocrine testing in typical obesity cases, as this can lead to false thyroid diagnoses and unnecessary delays in care. Red-flag comorbidities requiring urgent attention should be identified during this visit. These include hypertensive urgency with confirmed stage 2-range blood pressure, symptoms of overt diabetes with a random glucose level ≥200 mg/dL, suspected slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) presenting as hip or knee pain with a limp, severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) with significant daytime impairment, or moderate-to-severe depression, suicidality, or a suspected eating disorder. Finally, plan for comorbidity screening using a staged approach with conditional “if abnormal, then do” protocols. Set one achievable diet goal and one behavior goal that can be monitored at home, and schedule follow-up within 2–4 weeks to assess adherence to the initial home-based lifestyle therapy (IHBLT) and its dosing.

case_studies.title: Visit-One Diagnostic Confirmation in a 12-Year-Old with Suspected Obesity
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy presents to a paediatrics outpatient clinic in a semi-urban Indian town with concerns about excessive weight gain over the past two years. His mother reports that he has become less active and prefers screen time over outdoor play. She also mentions occasional knee discomfort but denies any history of trauma. There is no family history of endocrine disorders, but both parents are overweight. On examination, the child appears visibly overweight with a height of 140 cm and weight of 52 kg. His BMI is calculated at 26.5 kg/m² and plotted on the IAP BMI chart, showing obesity. Blood pressure is measured twice, with readings of 134/88 mmHg (stage 1 hypertension). No cushingoid features, acanthosis nigricans, or significant joint abnormalities are noted. The child denies symptoms of excessive hunger or fatigue. A random blood glucose test is performed in the clinic and is 140 mg/dL. The family is concerned about his future health and seeks advice on next steps.
case_studies.learning_objective: Confirm obesity using growth charts and BMI calculations, differentiate primary obesity from secondary causes, and identify red-flag comorbidities requiring urgent escalation or targeted referrals.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SAME-DAY REFERRAL CRITERIA FOR EMERGENCY COMORBIDITY PRESENTATIONS
subtopics.category: SEQUENCED OUTPATIENT PATHWAY AND FOLLOW-UP CADENCE
subtopics.concept: Same-day referral for emergency comorbidity presentations in children with obesity is critical to address time-sensitive complications and avoid delayed care or medicolegal risks. Immediate referral to emergency care is warranted for signs of acute metabolic decompensation, such as polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing, altered sensorium, or markedly elevated capillary glucose levels suggestive of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Outpatient laboratory tests should not delay this process. Similarly, hypertensive emergencies require urgent attention, particularly if severe symptomatic hypertension presents with a confirmed very high blood pressure or associated symptoms like severe headache, chest pain, dyspnea, neurologic deficits, or visual changes. Mental health emergencies also necessitate same-day psychiatric evaluation. These include suicidal ideation with a plan or intent, self-harm behaviors, severe depression impairing functionality, or suspected abuse, all of which require immediate safety planning. For suspected eating disorders with medical instability, such as syncope, severe bradycardia, postural hypotension, dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities, or rapid weight loss, stabilization must take precedence over weight-management counseling. Orthopedic and neurologic red flags also demand urgent action. Acute hip or knee pain associated with a limp or inability to bear weight may indicate slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), necessitating prompt imaging, orthopedic referral, and non-weight-bearing instructions. Similarly, a new headache with visual symptoms or suspected papilledema raises concern for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) and requires urgent neuroimaging and ophthalmologic evaluation. To ensure continuity of care, it is essential to document the trigger symptoms, objective findings, and the referral pathway utilized, whether it involves the emergency department, a same-day specialty clinic, or hospital admission. This approach minimizes redundant screening during subsequent visits and ensures appropriate follow-up care.

case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy from a semi-urban area presents to the outpatient clinic with a 2-day history of excessive thirst, frequent urination, and generalized fatigue. His mother notes that he has been vomiting since the previous evening and appears increasingly drowsy. On examination, the child is lethargic with deep, labored breathing. His heart rate is 110 bpm, respiratory rate is 28/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg. Capillary glucose measured in the clinic is 450 mg/dL. The child also reports vague abdominal pain but is unable to provide clear details due to his altered mental state. His BMI is >95th percentile for age and gender. The family mentions a history of obesity and type 2 diabetes in close relatives. No prior medical history of diabetes is documented for the child.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VISITS-TWO-THROUGH-FOUR MODULE DELIVERY AND BARRIER SOLVING
subtopics.category: SEQUENCED OUTPATIENT PATHWAY AND FOLLOW-UP CADENCE
subtopics.concept: In the sequenced outpatient pathway for managing overnutrition and obesity in paediatric patients, visits two through four are critical for determining outcomes. A common pitfall during these visits is the repetition of generic advice, which is ineffective. Instead, clinicians should implement an escalating, measurable program with a fixed agenda at each visit. Begin by recording key measurements, including weight at every visit, height periodically, BMI trajectory, and blood pressure. Additionally, include one process indicator that reflects progress from the prior module, such as sugary beverages consumed per week, number of days breakfast is eaten, daily minutes of physical activity, or device-off time. Emphasize trajectory over single values, as weight maintenance with a decreasing BMI in growing children signifies meaningful progress. Each visit should focus on delivering one lifestyle module using a concise script, outlining two to three specific actions, and assigning concrete homework. Proper documentation of the module and homework is essential to later assess whether non-response stems from inadequate delivery or genuine treatment resistance. Identifying and addressing one barrier per visit is equally important. Barriers are often predictable, such as exam schedules, unsafe outdoor environments, late family meals, or bullying. Effective problem-solving requires clinical skill rather than motivational dialogue. Conclude each visit by linking new information to actionable steps. For example, review comorbidity results and initiate the next step, such as repeat testing, referral, or targeted management, rather than merely repeating screening. Within the Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) staged framework, escalate care intensity from prevention-plus to structured weight management and eventually to multidisciplinary models only after confirming that foundational modules have been delivered with reasonable fidelity. This structured and systematic approach ensures that visits are productive and tailored to the individual needs of paediatric patients, promoting sustainable progress in managing overnutrition and obesity.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SIX-TO-TWELVE-MONTH ESCALATION THRESHOLDS AND REFERRAL TRIGGERS
subtopics.category: SEQUENCED OUTPATIENT PATHWAY AND FOLLOW-UP CADENCE
subtopics.concept: In the six-to-twelve-month period of managing overnutrition and obesity in paediatric patients, escalation thresholds and referral triggers must be carefully reassessed to determine whether lifestyle-only interventions remain adequate or if intensified care is required. A common error during this stage is underescalation, where low-intensity counseling is continued despite persistent severe obesity and worsening complications. According to the IAP framework, adolescents aged 12 years or older with class 2 obesity and immediate or life-threatening comorbidities, or class 3 obesity with or without comorbidities, are eligible for adjunct pharmacotherapy as part of comprehensive care. However, pharmacotherapy should only be initiated after a documented trial of structured lifestyle treatment, as drug therapy without a lifestyle foundation has poor long-term efficacy. Surgical referral is supported for children older than 12 years with class 2 obesity plus comorbidities or class 3 obesity with or without comorbidities, but only after failure of intense lifestyle modification and pharmacotherapy for at least six months. Such referrals should occur within experienced multidisciplinary centers, with global guidance emphasizing early evaluation and preparation to prevent irreversible comorbidities. Even when pharmacotherapy or surgery is being considered, lifestyle care should not be discontinued. Escalation must be accompanied by a higher-contact model involving a dietitian, activity specialist, psychological support (where available), and active comorbidity management. At the six-to-twelve-month milestone visit, the problem list should be updated to include the severity category, comorbidities, actions taken, lifestyle modules delivered, and the next escalation decision. A written management plan is crucial to ensure continuity of care, particularly when families transition between providers or from paediatric to adult services. Not specified in the source are the criteria for admission and escalation based on vitals, dehydration, neurological status, or bleeding, as well as initial labs, stabilization steps, thresholds for PICU, and discharge and review triggers.

case_studies.title: Persistent Severe Obesity in a 12-Year-Old with Escalation Needs
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy from a semi-urban setting in India presents for a follow-up visit after six months of lifestyle intervention for obesity. His parents report minimal improvement despite adherence to dietary changes and increased physical activity. Over the past three months, he has developed daytime fatigue, frequent headaches, and difficulty concentrating in school. His BMI is 38 kg/m², placing him in the class 3 obesity category. Focused examination reveals acanthosis nigricans on the neck and axillae, mild hypertension (blood pressure: 130/85 mmHg), and no signs of acute distress. A fasting blood glucose test from the previous week shows 126 mg/dL, and lipid profile results reveal elevated triglycerides (210 mg/dL). The family expresses frustration over the lack of progress and asks if additional treatments are available. The physician reviews the patient's progress and considers whether further escalation is warranted in the context of worsening complications and persistent severe obesity.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize escalation thresholds and referral triggers for paediatric obesity management at the six-to-twelve-month mark, including indications for pharmacotherapy and surgical evaluation in the presence of class 3 obesity and comorbidities.

case_studies.title: Escalation Thresholds in Persistent Severe Obesity with Comorbidities
case_studies.vignette: A 12-year-old boy from a semi-urban area in India presents for his six-month follow-up for obesity management. His initial BMI was 33 kg/m² (class 2 obesity), and he was started on structured lifestyle interventions, including dietary modifications and increased physical activity. Despite adherence to the program, his BMI has increased to 36 kg/m² (class 3 obesity). His mother reports worsening fatigue and difficulty breathing during mild exertion over the past two months. Focused examination reveals acanthosis nigricans on the neck and axillae, mild hepatomegaly, and elevated blood pressure (135/90 mmHg). Objective data from the previous visit included fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL and ALT levels at 65 U/L, suggestive of early metabolic derangements. His school performance has declined due to frequent absenteeism, and his family expresses concern about his mental health. The primary care team is uncertain whether lifestyle measures alone remain sufficient and seeks guidance on escalation and referral thresholds.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify escalation and referral triggers in paediatric obesity management at the six-to-twelve-month milestone, particularly in cases of worsening BMI and emerging comorbidities.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PHARMACOTHERAPY ELIGIBILITY, BASELINE ASSESSMENT, AND STOPPING RULES
subtopics.concept: Pharmacotherapy for pediatric obesity serves as an adjunct to structured lifestyle interventions and is not intended as a standalone treatment. It is recommended when severe obesity or serious comorbidities pose immediate health risks, particularly in cases where documented lifestyle programs have failed to yield adequate results. According to the 2023 IAP obesity guidelines, pharmacotherapy is appropriate for adolescents aged 12 years or older with class 2 obesity and life-threatening comorbidities or class 3 obesity regardless of comorbidities. Similarly, the AAP guidelines endorse weight-loss pharmacotherapy for adolescents aged 12 years or older with obesity as part of a comprehensive lifestyle treatment plan. Prior to initiating pharmacotherapy, a thorough baseline assessment is essential to ensure safety and adherence. This includes confirming the severity category based on chart documentation, identifying comorbidities, reviewing current medications, evaluating contraindications, and considering family history relevant to the chosen drug. Baseline measurements should include blood pressure, glucose or HbA1c, lipids, and ALT levels. Additionally, screening for depression, suicidality, and disordered eating is critical, as active eating disorders or unstable mental health may render pharmacotherapy unsafe or ineffective. For post-menarcheal females, pregnancy risk assessment and counseling are mandatory components of safe prescribing practices. Monitoring should be structured from the outset, with follow-up scheduled at 4–6 weeks to evaluate tolerability and adherence, and again at 12 weeks to assess therapeutic response. The IAP guidelines define pharmacotherapy as ineffective if it fails to safely reduce BMI by approximately 5% after 12 weeks of consistent use, necessitating reassessment of adherence, adverse effects, diagnosis, and potential escalation strategies. If significant adverse effects occur, treatment should be stopped or switched earlier. Specific stopping rules, including thresholds for discontinuation based on adverse effects or lack of response, are integral to ensuring patient safety and optimizing outcomes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ORLISTAT, METFORMIN, AND GLP-1 AGONIST DOSING WITH SAFETY MONITORING
subtopics.concept: In the pharmacological management of paediatric overnutrition and obesity, the selection of medications such as orlistat, metformin, and GLP-1 receptor agonists should align with the patient’s phenotype, comorbidities, and the family’s capacity for monitoring, and must always complement structured lifestyle interventions. Orlistat, an intestinal lipase inhibitor, is prescribed at 120 mg orally three times daily with each main fat-containing meal or within one hour after. Common adverse effects include steatorrhea, abdominal cramps, and fecal urgency, which can be exacerbated by high-fat meals. Patients should be counselled on dietary modifications, and a multivitamin containing fat-soluble vitamins should be taken at a different time to prevent deficiencies. GLP-1 receptor agonists, such as liraglutide and semaglutide, are specialist-prescribed options for adolescents. Liraglutide is initiated at 0.6 mg subcutaneously daily for one week, with weekly increments of 0.6 mg up to a maintenance dose of 3.0 mg daily, allowing slower escalation if tolerability issues arise. Semaglutide is administered weekly, starting at 0.25 mg for four weeks, then titrated to 0.5 mg, 1.0 mg, 1.7 mg, and finally 2.4 mg weekly as maintenance, with dose escalation delayed if adverse effects occur. The STEP TEENS trial demonstrated a mean BMI reduction of approximately 16% over 68 weeks with semaglutide 2.4 mg weekly combined with lifestyle interventions. However, gastrointestinal side effects, dehydration, gallbladder disease, and rare pancreatitis are potential risks. Semaglutide is contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. Weight trajectory, tolerability, and comorbidity markers should be closely monitored during treatment. Metformin, while not a primary anti-obesity agent, is beneficial for adolescents with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), high-risk prediabetes, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) with dysglycemia, or antipsychotic-associated weight gain. It is initiated at 500 mg once daily with food and titrated to 1000 mg twice daily as tolerated. Gastrointestinal side effects and the rare risk of lactic acidosis necessitate counselling, along with monitoring renal function and vitamin B12 levels during long-term use. Across all agents, explicit response targets and stopping rules should guide therapy, with reassessment of adherence and treatment reconsideration if a safe response is not achieved within approximately 12 weeks at a therapeutic dose.
